findings.title: fetchProjects useCallback missing hasPermission dependency — stale permission closure
findings.description: The useCallback at line 85 closes over `hasPermission` (line 101) but the dependency array at line 130 only lists `[user, memberMeFilter, showArchived]`. If the user's permissions change (e.g. after a session refresh or role change), `fetchProjects` will not be re-created and will keep using the stale closure over the old `hasPermission` function. The project fetch scope (getAll vs getByUser) depends entirely on the result of `hasPermission('projects:read')`, so a stale closure here will silently return wrong data.
findings.root_cause: `hasPermission` is used inside a `useCallback` but omitted from its dependency array, causing the callback to close over a potentially stale reference to the permission-checking function.
findings.impact: After a permission reload (e.g. role change or token refresh), the page continues to use the old permission check to decide which API call to make, potentially showing more or fewer projects than the user is entitled to see.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `hasPermission` to the `useCallback` dependency array: `}, [user, memberMeFilter, showArchived, hasPermission]);`. Since `hasPermission` comes from `usePermissions()`, verify that the hook returns a stable reference (memoized with `useCallback`); if not, stabilize it at the hook level first.
acceptance_criteria: ESLint react-hooks/exhaustive-deps rule passes without disable comments on this callback
After a mock permission change, fetchProjects re-runs and returns the correct scope
findings.verification_command: cd /home/alex/Documents/REPO/ORCHESTRA && npx eslint apps/web/app/\[locale\]/projects/page.tsx --rule '{"react-hooks/exhaustive-deps": "error"}' 2>&1 | grep fetchProjects
findings.notes: Same pattern is present in tasks/page.tsx — see frontend-F2-2.

findings.title: fetchData useCallback in tasks/page.tsx missing hasPermission dependency — stale permission closure
findings.description: `fetchData` uses `hasPermission` at lines 60, 62, 87, 104, and 117 to decide which API calls to make and which data sets to load, but `hasPermission` is absent from the `useCallback` dependency array `[user, assigneeMeFilter]` at line 144. This is the same stale-closure pattern as in projects/page.tsx (finding F2-1) but more severe here because `hasPermission` gates five different branches: projects:read, tasks:readAll, assignee-only, tasks:create (orphans), tasks:update+users:read.
findings.root_cause: `hasPermission` is referenced inside `useCallback` but omitted from its deps, capturing the value from initial render and never updating if permissions change.
findings.impact: Following a role change or permission reload, the tasks page will continue fetching data according to the old role's access level. An OBSERVATEUR promoted to ADMIN would still only see their own tasks until the page is fully remounted.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `hasPermission` to the dependency array: `}, [user, assigneeMeFilter, hasPermission]);`. Ensure `usePermissions` returns a stable `hasPermission` reference.
acceptance_criteria: ESLint react-hooks/exhaustive-deps passes on this callback without disable comment
Permission change causes fetchData to re-run with the new permission set
findings.verification_command: cd /home/alex/Documents/REPO/ORCHESTRA && npx eslint apps/web/app/\[locale\]/tasks/page.tsx --rule '{"react-hooks/exhaustive-deps": "error"}' 2>&1 | grep fetchData

findings.title: handleUpdateProject has no error handling — silent failure on project update
findings.description: `handleUpdateProject` awaits two API calls (`projectsService.update` and `projectsService.getById`) with no try/catch. If either call throws (network error, 403, 422 validation), the promise rejects and the error propagates uncaught to the caller (ProjectEditModal's `onSave`). Since ProjectEditModal likely does not wrap this in its own try/catch (it calls `onSave` which it receives as a prop), the user sees no feedback and the modal may remain open or close silently depending on the modal's own error handling.
findings.root_cause: No try/catch around async API calls in `handleUpdateProject`, unlike every other mutation handler in this file which all have try/catch blocks.
findings.impact: When a project update fails (e.g. validation error, network outage), the user receives no error toast and the UI may be left in an inconsistent state (edit modal still open, stale project data).
acceptance_criteria: A simulated 422 from projectsService.update triggers an error toast
The edit modal remains open after a failed save

findings.title: onArchive / onUnarchive in projects/page.tsx have no error handling
findings.description: Both `onArchive` and `onUnarchive` are async functions that await API calls (`projectsService.archive`, `projectsService.unarchive`) without any try/catch. On failure, the unhandled rejection propagates silently — the user receives no error feedback, and no UI update occurs (no toast, no re-render). This contrasts with all other mutation handlers in the same file which use toast.error on catch.
findings.root_cause: Missing try/catch in async event handlers that perform network mutations.
findings.impact: If archiving or unarchiving fails (network error, permission denied, server error), the user clicks the button and nothing happens with no feedback. The project list is also not refreshed if fetchProjects itself throws.
acceptance_criteria: A simulated API error on archive displays a toast.error
A simulated API error on unarchive displays a toast.error

findings.title: milestonesService.getAll() fetches ALL milestones system-wide then filters client-side in two pages
findings.description: Both `apps/web/app/[locale]/projects/[id]/page.tsx` (line 182-185) and `apps/web/app/[locale]/tasks/[id]/page.tsx` (line 97-101) call `milestonesService.getAll()` to retrieve all milestones in the system, then filter the result in JavaScript to find only those belonging to the current project. The milestones service already exposes `getByProject(projectId)` which returns only the relevant milestones. This pattern wastes bandwidth and API compute proportionally to the number of projects in the system.
findings.root_cause: Developers used the generic `getAll()` endpoint instead of the scoped `getByProject(projectId)` endpoint that already exists in the milestones service.
findings.impact: In a deployment with N projects, each page load of a project detail or task detail page downloads the milestone data for all N projects. For large deployments this adds significant latency and unnecessary server load. It also means the client must load data the user has no right to see (milestones from other projects the user may not have access to).
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace `milestonesService.getAll()` with `milestonesService.getByProject(projectId)` in both files:
- `apps/web/app/[locale]/projects/[id]/page.tsx` lines 182-185: replace with `const projectMilestones = await milestonesService.getByProject(projectId);` and `setMilestones(projectMilestones);`
- `apps/web/app/[locale]/tasks/[id]/page.tsx` lines 97-101: same replacement using `taskData.projectId`
acceptance_criteria: Network tab shows only one milestones request per page load scoped to the project
milestonesService.getByProject is called instead of getAll in both pages
No regression in milestone display in project detail and task detail pages
findings.notes: The same pattern exists in tasks/[id]/page.tsx at line 97. milestonesService.getByProject() is confirmed to exist at line 17 of apps/web/src/services/milestones.service.ts.

findings.title: tasksService.getAll(1, 1000) hard-coded limit of 1000 tasks loaded in single request
findings.description: When the user has `tasks:readAll` permission, the tasks page requests up to 1000 tasks in a single API call. This bypasses any server-side pagination. In an organisation with many tasks this will: (1) cause long API response times, (2) transmit a large payload over the network, (3) force React to render a potentially very large list client-side. All 1000 tasks are then filtered client-side by project/priority/overdue flags with no virtual rendering.
findings.root_cause: A hard-coded limit of 1000 was used as a workaround for pagination complexity, treating the API as if it were a bulk data source.
findings.impact: For deployments with hundreds or thousands of tasks, admin users will experience slow page loads and potentially browser jank. The server is also under unnecessary load for every page visit by a privileged user.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement server-side filtering by passing current filter params (project, priority, status, overdue) to the API, or implement virtual scrolling / progressive loading. At minimum, add a pagination UI or reduce the limit to 200 with a 'load more' affordance.
acceptance_criteria: The tasks page does not request more than 200 tasks per initial load for readAll users
Filters are passed as API query params rather than filtered client-side

findings.title: No error boundary wrapping any of the three page-level components
findings.description: None of the three pages (projects/page.tsx, tasks/page.tsx, planning/page.tsx) or their parent layouts are wrapped in a React Error Boundary. An unhandled render-time error in any child component (e.g. unexpected null from the API, a bug in a component reading task.project.name when project is null) will result in a blank white screen for the user with no actionable message. The planning page in particular delegates entirely to the dynamically loaded PlanningView component with no fallback.
findings.root_cause: No Error Boundary component was added to these routes at the Next.js page level or the MainLayout level.
findings.impact: Any uncaught render error in projects, tasks, or planning pages will crash the entire page for the user with no recovery path and no user-facing error message.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Next.js `error.tsx` (App Router convention) next to each `page.tsx`, or wrap `<MainLayout>` in a shared `<ErrorBoundary fallback={...}>` component that displays a user-friendly error message and a retry button.
acceptance_criteria: Throwing an error from a child component inside MainLayout displays an error fallback UI instead of a blank page
The error is reported (via logger or monitoring service)
findings.notes: Applies equally to all three target directories.

findings.title: planning/page.tsx is entirely a shell that could be an RSC with a single client import
findings.description: The planning page is marked `"use client"` but does not use any client-only hooks (useState, useEffect, useRef, etc.) itself. It is a pure pass-through shell around a dynamically imported component. If `MainLayout` is also an RSC (or can be made one), this page does not need to be a Client Component — removing `"use client"` from the shell would allow Next.js to server-render the outer layout and only hydrate PlanningView on the client, reducing the client JS bundle for the shell.
findings.root_cause: The `"use client"` directive was added prophylactically or because the developer was unsure if it was needed.
findings.impact: Minor: the page shell is included in the client JS bundle unnecessarily. This increases Time To Interactive slightly for users on slow connections.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `"use client"` from `planning/page.tsx`. If `MainLayout` requires client context, ensure PlanningView is imported with `dynamic` and `ssr: false` (already done). Verify the build still works and the page renders correctly.
acceptance_criteria: Removing `"use client"` from planning/page.tsx does not cause a build error
PlanningPage renders correctly in production build
findings.notes: Depends on whether MainLayout itself requires client context. If MainLayout uses useRouter or auth store, it's already a client component and this optimization is moot.

findings.title: fetchThirdPartyMembers and fetchProjectClients close over canRead* flags but effect deps are suppressed
findings.description: Both useEffect hooks for tab-driven data fetching use `eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` to suppress the exhaustive-deps rule. The functions `fetchThirdPartyMembers` and `fetchProjectClients` close over `canReadThirdParties`/`canReadClients` and `projectId`, which change only on initial load in practice. However, the pattern hides potential staleness if permissions reload. Additionally, defining async helper functions outside useCallback and referencing them inside effects is an anti-pattern that can lead to stale captures as the component evolves.
findings.root_cause: Helper functions were defined as plain async functions outside useCallback, making ESLint require them as deps. The `disable` comment was used instead of refactoring to useCallback or inlining the logic.
findings.impact: Low in practice (permissions rarely change mid-session), but the eslint-disable comment hides any future staleness issues introduced by extending these functions.
findings.suggested_fix: Either wrap `fetchThirdPartyMembers` and `fetchProjectClients` in `useCallback` with the correct deps, or inline the logic directly inside the effects and remove the eslint-disable comments.
acceptance_criteria: No eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps comment on these two effects
Third-party and clients tabs still fetch data correctly on tab switch

verified_clean: No dangerouslySetInnerHTML usage found in any scanned file
No secrets or API tokens hardcoded in client-side source files
No open redirect: all router.push() calls use locale-prefixed internal paths constructed from controlled variables

Auth decisions (canEditProject, canDeleteProject, canEdit, canCreateProject, canCreateTask) are all derived from hasPermission() which reads from the RBAC permission store — no client-side role string comparison
No console.log with PII: all debug logging goes through the logger utility imported from @/lib/logger
Optimistic updates: no optimistic update pattern is used — all mutations await the API call before updating state (safe but slower)
Locale/i18n routing: useLocale() used consistently for locale prefix; no manual locale string hardcoding
CSP nonce: pages use no inline scripts or style injection that would require nonce handling
localStorage token exposure: auth state is in Zustand store (memory), not directly read/written in these pages
GanttChart is lazy-loaded with dynamic() and ssr:false — correct for a heavy client-side component
PlanningView is lazy-loaded with dynamic() and ssr:false — correct for a calendar/planning component

tasks/[id]/page.tsx: handleSave, handleDelete, handleDependencyChange all have try/catch with user-facing error toasts
